ns-company,"{""name"":""CÚRADOR"",""tagline"":""Quality. Craft. Culture."",""ethos"":""CÚRADOR is a Missouri-licensed cannabis manufacturing company operating under a house of brands model — awarded a manufacturing license in the first round of Missouri medical cannabis licensing. Operating as a micro-scale manufacturing lab, CÚRADOR competes not on volume but on quality, execution, and cultural credibility. The company's philosophy is rooted in the belief that craft craftsmanship and internal culture are sustainable competitive advantages in saturated markets: smaller batches, higher standards, and brands built through reputation rather than hype. CÚRADOR also manufactures and distributes AiroPro, a third-party licensed brand using proprietary cartridge technology, providing additional manufacturing throughput and operational leverage."",""mission"":""Sustainable growth through reputation, consistency, and disciplined brand management — building brands that earn long-term consumer trust across every tier of the Missouri cannabis market."",""values"":[""Craft Over Volume"",""Brand Stewardship"",""Operational Consistency"",""Cultural Credibility"",""Disciplined Portfolio Management""],""model"":""Hybrid: proprietary brand development (Headchange, SafeBet, Bubbles) + licensed manufacturing & distribution (AiroPro). Each brand serves a distinct consumer segment with no internal overlap."",""context"":""Missouri's cannabis market is highly competitive — characterized by rapid brand proliferation, price compression, and increasing pressure to differentiate beyond packaging. CÚRADOR's response: quality-driven manufacturing, not commodity production.""}",2026-04-07 14:25:11.57+00
<title>Curador — House of Brands · QR Journey Concept</title>
One scan. One batch. Three brands. One ecosystem.

Every Curador product carries a single batch-specific QR code. It does three jobs in sequence: authenticates the product, surfaces COA data, and opens a loyalty capture moment — all in one scan, on a page unique to that batch.

01
Printed QR on Testing Label
Every product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. This is the confirmed touchpoint across all brands and all SKUs — no exceptions, no additional workflow. Batch-level code, routes to brand site, opens loyalty flow.

02
DCR Compliance
The testing label QR satisfies DCR's ""learn more about the product"" requirement. It routes to the brand's strain/flavor card with COA, potency, and terpene data for that batch.

03
First-Party Data
After landing on the strain card, a loyalty Join CTA and newsletter pop-up capture consumer data. Cookie-on members auto-log. No-cookie members see a sign-in prompt. One scan, one flow.

04
Unique Per-Unit QR — Upgrade Path
If in-house printing can support variable-data QR generation per unit, each product gets a unique code tied to that specific SKU. Enables unit-level authentication and richer loyalty tracking. Confirmed feasibility needed first.

column2: >Confirmed — Printed QR on testing label (all products): Every Curador product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. It routes to the brand site, surfaces COA data, and opens the loyalty flow. Batch-level code — the same QR for all units in a production run.

>Tier 2 — Per-product ID sticker (conditional upgrade): A separate sticker printed with a unique product-level QR code — one per individual unit, not per batch — enables unit-level authentication and tracking. This requires state approval and a logistics solution for per-unit sticker generation and application. If achievable, it is the superior experience. If not, Tier 1 already does the job.

column1: Before finalising the QR print strategy, two teams need answers. These questions are grouped by who owns them. The goal: find the path that maximises consumer journey without straining the packaging department.

column5: >Can we apply a sticker printed with the batch QR and strain name to the inside of the box top flap — visible the moment the consumer opens the box, positioned directly above the jar?

column4: >This keeps the exterior packaging clean, gives the QR generous real estate (the full flap width), and creates a natural discovery moment at first open. The consumer lifts the flap, sees strain name + QR, scans immediately before they even touch the jar. Key question: does DCR consider an inner flap sticker ""accessible to the consumer"" for the purpose of the product information QR requirement, and does it count as primary or secondary packaging surface?

column5: >Can we print the strain name in a branded typeface directly beneath the QR code on any packaging surface — lid label, testing label, or box sticker?

column4: >DCR restricts logos and imagery near QR codes but text labelling the product may be permitted. Clarify whether the strain name constitutes required labelling (always allowed) or discretionary branding (may require review). If permitted, this significantly improves scannability — consumers know what they're scanning before they point the camera.

column5: >What are the minimum and maximum allowable QR code dimensions under DCR, and does the regulation specify any quiet zone (white border) requirements?

column4: >A QR printed too small won't scan reliably, especially on curved surfaces like jar lids. General best practice is 2cm × 2cm minimum for reliable phone scanning at arm's length. Larger QRs on flat box surfaces can go up to the full available label area. Confirm whether state rules impose a cap, and whether colour inversion (light QR on dark background) is permitted.

column7: >Q4 — Inner flap sticker workflow for jar concentrates

column5: >For Head Change jar concentrates, the proposed placement is a sticker on the inside of the box top flap — printed with the batch QR code and strain name, applied before the jar goes in the box. What does this add to the packaging workflow and per-unit cost?

column4: >The sticker is applied once per box before sealing — likely the same station where the testing label goes on. Since the QR is batch-generated anyway, the sticker sheet is printed per batch run. Key questions: what is the flap interior dimensions for the current Head Change jar box, can the sticker printer handle the batch QR generation inline, and does the current line have a clean station for inner-flap application before the jar is placed?

column7: >Q5 — Is the printed QR on the testing label large enough to scan?

column5: >The printed QR on the testing label is the confirmed baseline for all products. The practical question is whether it is printed at a size and in a position that a consumer would actually notice and be able to scan reliably.

column4: >Testing labels are dense with required compliance fields. The QR may be small and hard to find. If the existing QR is already 2×2cm or larger and in a visible position, Tier 1 is ready to go with no changes. If it is smaller or buried in compliance text, the label template may need to be updated to make the QR larger and more prominent — or an additional consumer-facing placement (like an inner flap sticker) needs to be considered alongside it.

column5: >Action: get a testing label sample from packaging

column4: >Measure the current QR print size and test scannability at arm's length on each product format. This determines whether Tier 1 is already consumer-ready or needs a placement upgrade.

column1: The print strategy is confirmed: <strong style=""color:var(--white)
column2: >printed QR on the testing label, all products, all brands. The QR must route to a page that can authenticate the purchase — which means a pre-printed box QR is not viable. A box QR encodes a static URL that cannot be tied to a specific unit or batch at the point of printing, and cannot authenticate the individual purchase. The testing label already carries batch-specific data, making it the only packaging surface where a meaningful QR can be printed. Two remaining open actions: (1) confirm the printed QR is large enough to scan reliably, and (2) assess whether variable-data printing is feasible for the per-unit upgrade.

column1: Two Ways to Verify. One Reward.

Consumers can verify their Curador purchase and earn loyalty points through two paths. Path A — scanning the testing label QR — is confirmed and operational. Path B — uploading a dispensary receipt — is a proposed upgrade path, pending confirmation that Missouri dispensary receipts include a product ID number that can be matched against Curador's product registry, and that OCR parsing of those receipts is reliable enough to automate verification.

column7: >Why receipt upload would matter — if confirmed

column4: >The testing label QR may be small or not obviously consumer-facing. Many buyers won't see it until they're already home. Receipt upload captures this group.
>Every legal cannabis sale generates a receipt. This is a universal proof-of-purchase touchpoint that exists regardless of QR placement, label size, or packaging format.

>Receipt uploads tell us which dispensaries are moving which products. This is first-party retail intelligence that QR scans can't provide — receipts show where the purchase happened.

column2: >Must confirm before receipt upload can proceed:

column3: >Do Missouri dispensary POS systems print a product ID number on receipts? This is the single most critical dependency. Without a product ID on the receipt, there is no reliable way to authenticate the specific product purchased — product name alone is too ambiguous and too easy to spoof. Talk to dispensary partners and pull sample receipts from multiple MO retailers to confirm.

>Can OCR reliably read that product ID from a phone photo of a receipt? Cannabis receipts vary significantly by POS system (Dutchie, Treez, Flowhub, BioTrack). Some print cleanly, some are thermal and fade. Test OCR accuracy across real receipt samples before committing to automated processing.

From Two QRs to One — Revised

The original Option D proposed two separate QRs: a compliance QR on the testing label and a marketing QR on the box. The current strategy supersedes this with a cleaner two-tier model. Tier 1 is the testing label QR — already on every product, batch-level, does compliance and loyalty in one scan. Tier 2 is a per-unit product ID sticker — a conditional upgrade if state permits and logistics allow, linking to an exact unit rather than a batch.

The key insight: the testing label QR is already unique per batch and already routes to the brand site. There is no need for a second generic marketing QR. The remaining decisions are (1) whether the testing label QR is large and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan, and (2) whether a Tier 2 per-unit product ID sticker is achievable — which would enable unit-level tracking and authentication beyond what a batch code can provide. Tier 1 ships. Tier 2 is pursued in parallel pending state and logistics confirmation.

>A strain-specific sticker QR solves visibility but adds a separate print run and another touchpoint in the packaging workflow for every small batch.

column4: >A box-printed QR is clean and bulk-ordered but can't point to a specific strain — only a broader SKU page.

column5: >Let the two QRs do different jobs. The testing label QR handles compliance — strain-specific, required anyway, done. The box-printed QR handles marketing — visible, bulk-friendly, always lands on the brand site.

>The box QR routes to a brand SKU overview page where the consumer selects their strain or flavor. Since they just bought the product, they know what they have — one tap to confirm is engagement, not friction.

column4: >Either way, the consumer lands on the brand site. Data capture opportunity exists on both paths.

column5: >The SKU overview page becomes a live surface — showing current available strains, seasonal drops, what's in stock right now. More useful than a static strain card for repeat buyers.

column6: >⚐ Requires confirmation that MO dispensary receipts include a scannable product ID.

column4: >Purchase verified — points awarded to loyalty account. Same earn as QR path. Receipt logged to prevent double-claiming.
>Post-purchase · When QR not scanned at point of sale · Existing customers discovering loyalty late

column7: >Why receipt upload would matter — if confirmed

column4: >The testing label QR may be small or not obviously consumer-facing. Many buyers won't see it until they're already home. Receipt upload captures this group.
>Every legal cannabis sale generates a receipt. This is a universal proof-of-purchase touchpoint that exists regardless of QR placement, label size, or packaging format.
>A customer who bought two weeks ago and just found the loyalty program can upload their receipt and still earn. QR scanning can't do this — the receipt can.
>Receipt uploads tell us which dispensaries are moving which products. This is first-party retail intelligence that QR scans can't provide — receipts show where the purchase happened.

column7: >Verification logic — how each path confirms a real purchase

column1: — Batch code in QR URL matches a registered Curador batch in backend
— Batch is marked as active (not recalled, not expired)
— Brand and product type confirmed from batch record

column2: >Tier 2: Unit code matched to a specific serialised product ID

column1: — Points awarded immediately on confirmed match

column2: >Product ID matched against Curador product registry — this is the authentication step. Product name alone is not sufficient.

column1: — Purchase date within valid claim window
— Receipt hash stored to prevent duplicate claims
— Points awarded on confirmed product ID match

column2: >⚐ Entire path depends on: (1) MO dispensary receipts including a product ID, and (2) OCR reliably reading that ID from a photo

column7: >Where the receipt upload CTA would appear — pending confirmation

column4: >Below the loyalty Join CTA — secondary option for anyone who has the product but didn't scan the QR at the right moment.
>Logged-in members can upload receipts for any past purchase within the claim window — retroactive earning for missed scans.
>Post sign-up comms include a ""missed a scan? upload your receipt"" CTA — captures purchases made before the consumer enrolled.
>Points redemption hub also surfaces receipt upload — consumer arrives to redeem and can top up their balance before checking out.

column2: >Must confirm before receipt upload can proceed:

column3: >Do Missouri dispensary POS systems print a product ID number on receipts? This is the single most critical dependency. Without a product ID on the receipt, there is no reliable way to authenticate the specific product purchased — product name alone is too ambiguous and too easy to spoof. Talk to dispensary partners and pull sample receipts from multiple MO retailers to confirm.

>Can OCR reliably read that product ID from a phone photo of a receipt? Cannabis receipts vary significantly by POS system (Dutchie, Treez, Flowhub, BioTrack). Some print cleanly, some are thermal and fade. Test OCR accuracy across real receipt samples before committing to automated processing.

column1: <title>Curador — House of Brands · QR Journey Concept</title>
One scan. One batch. Three brands. One ecosystem.

Every Curador product carries a single batch-specific QR code. It does three jobs in sequence: authenticates the product, surfaces COA data, and opens a loyalty capture moment — all in one scan, on a page unique to that batch.

01
Printed QR on Testing Label
Every product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. This is the confirmed touchpoint across all brands and all SKUs — no exceptions, no additional workflow. Batch-level code, routes to brand site, opens loyalty flow.

02
DCR Compliance
The testing label QR satisfies DCR's ""learn more about the product"" requirement. It routes to the brand's strain/flavor card with COA, potency, and terpene data for that batch.

03
First-Party Data
After landing on the strain card, a loyalty Join CTA and newsletter pop-up capture consumer data. Cookie-on members auto-log. No-cookie members see a sign-in prompt. One scan, one flow.

04
Unique Per-Unit QR — Upgrade Path
If in-house printing can support variable-data QR generation per unit, each product gets a unique code tied to that specific SKU. Enables unit-level authentication and richer loyalty tracking. Confirmed feasibility needed first.

column2: >Confirmed — Printed QR on testing label (all products): Every Curador product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. It routes to the brand site, surfaces COA data, and opens the loyalty flow. Batch-level code — the same QR for all units in a production run.

>Tier 2 — Per-product ID sticker (conditional upgrade): A separate sticker printed with a unique product-level QR code — one per individual unit, not per batch — enables unit-level authentication and tracking. This requires state approval and a logistics solution for per-unit sticker generation and application. If achievable, it is the superior experience. If not, Tier 1 already does the job.

column1: Before finalising the QR print strategy, two teams need answers. These questions are grouped by who owns them. The goal: find the path that maximises consumer journey without straining the packaging department.

column5: >Can we apply a sticker printed with the batch QR and strain name to the inside of the box top flap — visible the moment the consumer opens the box, positioned directly above the jar?

column4: >This keeps the exterior packaging clean, gives the QR generous real estate (the full flap width), and creates a natural discovery moment at first open. The consumer lifts the flap, sees strain name + QR, scans immediately before they even touch the jar. Key question: does DCR consider an inner flap sticker ""accessible to the consumer"" for the purpose of the product information QR requirement, and does it count as primary or secondary packaging surface?

column5: >Can we print the strain name in a branded typeface directly beneath the QR code on any packaging surface — lid label, testing label, or box sticker?

column4: >DCR restricts logos and imagery near QR codes but text labelling the product may be permitted. Clarify whether the strain name constitutes required labelling (always allowed) or discretionary branding (may require review). If permitted, this significantly improves scannability — consumers know what they're scanning before they point the camera.

column5: >What are the minimum and maximum allowable QR code dimensions under DCR, and does the regulation specify any quiet zone (white border) requirements?

column4: >A QR printed too small won't scan reliably, especially on curved surfaces like jar lids. General best practice is 2cm × 2cm minimum for reliable phone scanning at arm's length. Larger QRs on flat box surfaces can go up to the full available label area. Confirm whether state rules impose a cap, and whether colour inversion (light QR on dark background) is permitted.

column7: >Q4 — Inner flap sticker workflow for jar concentrates

column5: >For Head Change jar concentrates, the proposed placement is a sticker on the inside of the box top flap — printed with the batch QR code and strain name, applied before the jar goes in the box. What does this add to the packaging workflow and per-unit cost?

column4: >The sticker is applied once per box before sealing — likely the same station where the testing label goes on. Since the QR is batch-generated anyway, the sticker sheet is printed per batch run. Key questions: what is the flap interior dimensions for the current Head Change jar box, can the sticker printer handle the batch QR generation inline, and does the current line have a clean station for inner-flap application before the jar is placed?

column7: >Q5 — Is the printed QR on the testing label large enough to scan?

column5: >The printed QR on the testing label is the confirmed baseline for all products. The practical question is whether it is printed at a size and in a position that a consumer would actually notice and be able to scan reliably.

column4: >Testing labels are dense with required compliance fields. The QR may be small and hard to find. If the existing QR is already 2×2cm or larger and in a visible position, Tier 1 is ready to go with no changes. If it is smaller or buried in compliance text, the label template may need to be updated to make the QR larger and more prominent — or an additional consumer-facing placement (like an inner flap sticker) needs to be considered alongside it.

column5: >Action: get a testing label sample from packaging

column4: >Measure the current QR print size and test scannability at arm's length on each product format. This determines whether Tier 1 is already consumer-ready or needs a placement upgrade.

column1: The print strategy is confirmed: <strong style=""color:var(--white)
column2: >printed QR on the testing label, all products, all brands. The QR must route to a page that can authenticate the purchase — which means a pre-printed box QR is not viable. A box QR encodes a static URL that cannot be tied to a specific unit or batch at the point of printing, and cannot authenticate the individual purchase. The testing label already carries batch-specific data, making it the only packaging surface where a meaningful QR can be printed. Two remaining open actions: (1) confirm the printed QR is large enough to scan reliably, and (2) assess whether variable-data printing is feasible for the per-unit upgrade.

From Two QRs to One — Revised

The original Option D proposed two separate QRs: a compliance QR on the testing label and a marketing QR on the box. The current strategy supersedes this with a cleaner two-tier model. Tier 1 is the testing label QR — already on every product, batch-level, does compliance and loyalty in one scan. Tier 2 is a per-unit product ID sticker — a conditional upgrade if state permits and logistics allow, linking to an exact unit rather than a batch.

The key insight: the testing label QR is already unique per batch and already routes to the brand site. There is no need for a second generic marketing QR. The remaining decisions are (1) whether the testing label QR is large and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan, and (2) whether a Tier 2 per-unit product ID sticker is achievable — which would enable unit-level tracking and authentication beyond what a batch code can provide. Tier 1 ships. Tier 2 is pursued in parallel pending state and logistics confirmation.

column1: <title>Curador — House of Brands · QR Journey Concept</title>
One scan. One batch. Three brands. One ecosystem.

Every Curador product carries a single batch-specific QR code. It does three jobs in sequence: authenticates the product, surfaces COA data, and opens a loyalty capture moment — all in one scan, on a page unique to that batch.

01
Printed QR on Testing Label
Every product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. This is the confirmed touchpoint across all brands and all SKUs — no exceptions, no additional workflow. Batch-level code, routes to brand site, opens loyalty flow.

02
DCR Compliance
The testing label QR satisfies DCR's ""learn more about the product"" requirement. It routes to the brand's strain/flavor card with COA, potency, and terpene data for that batch.

03
First-Party Data
After landing on the strain card, a loyalty Join CTA and newsletter pop-up capture consumer data. Cookie-on members auto-log. No-cookie members see a sign-in prompt. One scan, one flow.

04
Unique Per-Unit QR — Upgrade Path
If in-house printing can support variable-data QR generation per unit, each product gets a unique code tied to that specific SKU. Enables unit-level authentication and richer loyalty tracking. Confirmed feasibility needed first.

column2: >Confirmed — Printed QR on testing label (all products): Every Curador product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. It routes to the brand site, surfaces COA data, and opens the loyalty flow. Batch-level code — the same QR for all units in a production run.

>Tier 2 — Per-product ID sticker (conditional upgrade): A separate sticker printed with a unique product-level QR code — one per individual unit, not per batch — enables unit-level authentication and tracking. This requires state approval and a logistics solution for per-unit sticker generation and application. If achievable, it is the superior experience. If not, Tier 1 already does the job.

column1: Before finalising the QR print strategy, two teams need answers. These questions are grouped by who owns them. The goal: find the path that maximises consumer journey without straining the packaging department.

column5: >Can we apply a sticker printed with the batch QR and strain name to the inside of the box top flap — visible the moment the consumer opens the box, positioned directly above the jar?

column4: >This keeps the exterior packaging clean, gives the QR generous real estate (the full flap width), and creates a natural discovery moment at first open. The consumer lifts the flap, sees strain name + QR, scans immediately before they even touch the jar. Key question: does DCR consider an inner flap sticker ""accessible to the consumer"" for the purpose of the product information QR requirement, and does it count as primary or secondary packaging surface?

column5: >Can we print the strain name in a branded typeface directly beneath the QR code on any packaging surface — lid label, testing label, or box sticker?

column4: >DCR restricts logos and imagery near QR codes but text labelling the product may be permitted. Clarify whether the strain name constitutes required labelling (always allowed) or discretionary branding (may require review). If permitted, this significantly improves scannability — consumers know what they're scanning before they point the camera.

column5: >What are the minimum and maximum allowable QR code dimensions under DCR, and does the regulation specify any quiet zone (white border) requirements?

column4: >A QR printed too small won't scan reliably, especially on curved surfaces like jar lids. General best practice is 2cm × 2cm minimum for reliable phone scanning at arm's length. Larger QRs on flat box surfaces can go up to the full available label area. Confirm whether state rules impose a cap, and whether colour inversion (light QR on dark background) is permitted.

column7: >Q4 — Inner flap sticker workflow for jar concentrates

column5: >For Head Change jar concentrates, the proposed placement is a sticker on the inside of the box top flap — printed with the batch QR code and strain name, applied before the jar goes in the box. What does this add to the packaging workflow and per-unit cost?

column4: >The sticker is applied once per box before sealing — likely the same station where the testing label goes on. Since the QR is batch-generated anyway, the sticker sheet is printed per batch run. Key questions: what is the flap interior dimensions for the current Head Change jar box, can the sticker printer handle the batch QR generation inline, and does the current line have a clean station for inner-flap application before the jar is placed?

column7: >Q5 — Is the printed QR on the testing label large enough to scan?

column5: >The printed QR on the testing label is the confirmed baseline for all products. The practical question is whether it is printed at a size and in a position that a consumer would actually notice and be able to scan reliably.

column4: >Testing labels are dense with required compliance fields. The QR may be small and hard to find. If the existing QR is already 2×2cm or larger and in a visible position, Tier 1 is ready to go with no changes. If it is smaller or buried in compliance text, the label template may need to be updated to make the QR larger and more prominent — or an additional consumer-facing placement (like an inner flap sticker) needs to be considered alongside it.

column5: >Action: get a testing label sample from packaging

column4: >Measure the current QR print size and test scannability at arm's length on each product format. This determines whether Tier 1 is already consumer-ready or needs a placement upgrade.

column1: The print strategy is confirmed: <strong style=""color:var(--white)
column2: >printed QR on the testing label, all products, all brands. The QR must route to a page that can authenticate the purchase — which means a pre-printed box QR is not viable. A box QR encodes a static URL that cannot be tied to a specific unit or batch at the point of printing, and cannot authenticate the individual purchase. The testing label already carries batch-specific data, making it the only packaging surface where a meaningful QR can be printed. Two remaining open actions: (1) confirm the printed QR is large enough to scan reliably, and (2) assess whether variable-data printing is feasible for the per-unit upgrade.

column1: Two Ways to Verify. One Reward.

Consumers can verify their Curador purchase and earn loyalty points through two paths. Path A — scanning the testing label QR — is confirmed and operational. Path B — uploading a dispensary receipt — is a proposed upgrade path, pending confirmation that Missouri dispensary receipts include a product ID number that can be matched against Curador's product registry, and that OCR parsing of those receipts is reliable enough to automate verification.

Head Change · Safe Bet · Bubbles
Draft v1.2 · Revised for Batch Authentication
From Two QRs to One — Revised

The original Option D proposed two separate QRs: a compliance QR on the testing label and a marketing QR on the box. The current strategy supersedes this with a cleaner two-tier model. Tier 1 is the testing label QR — already on every product, batch-level, does compliance and loyalty in one scan. Tier 2 is a per-unit product ID sticker — a conditional upgrade if state permits and logistics allow, linking to an exact unit rather than a batch.

The key insight: the testing label QR is already unique per batch and already routes to the brand site. There is no need for a second generic marketing QR. The remaining decisions are (1) whether the testing label QR is large and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan, and (2) whether a Tier 2 per-unit product ID sticker is achievable — which would enable unit-level tracking and authentication beyond what a batch code can provide. Tier 1 ships. Tier 2 is pursued in parallel pending state and logistics confirmation.

column1: <title>Curador — House of Brands · QR Journey Concept</title>
One scan. One batch. Three brands. One ecosystem.

Every Curador product carries a single batch-specific QR code. It does three jobs in sequence: authenticates the product, surfaces COA data, and opens a loyalty capture moment — all in one scan, on a page unique to that batch.

01
Printed QR on Testing Label
Every product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. This is the confirmed touchpoint across all brands and all SKUs — no exceptions, no additional workflow. Batch-level code, routes to brand site, opens loyalty flow.

02
DCR Compliance
The testing label QR satisfies DCR's ""learn more about the product"" requirement. It routes to the brand's strain/flavor card with COA, potency, and terpene data for that batch.

03
First-Party Data
After landing on the strain card, a loyalty Join CTA and newsletter pop-up capture consumer data. Cookie-on members auto-log. No-cookie members see a sign-in prompt. One scan, one flow.

04
Unique Per-Unit QR — Upgrade Path
If in-house printing can support variable-data QR generation per unit, each product gets a unique code tied to that specific SKU. Enables unit-level authentication and richer loyalty tracking. Confirmed feasibility needed first.

column2: >Confirmed — Printed QR on testing label (all products): Every Curador product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. It routes to the brand site, surfaces COA data, and opens the loyalty flow. Batch-level code — the same QR for all units in a production run.

>Tier 2 — Per-product ID sticker (conditional upgrade): A separate sticker printed with a unique product-level QR code — one per individual unit, not per batch — enables unit-level authentication and tracking. This requires state approval and a logistics solution for per-unit sticker generation and application. If achievable, it is the superior experience. If not, Tier 1 already does the job.

column1: Before finalising the QR print strategy, two teams need answers. These questions are grouped by who owns them. The goal: find the path that maximises consumer journey without straining the packaging department.

column5: >Can we apply a sticker printed with the batch QR and strain name to the inside of the box top flap — visible the moment the consumer opens the box, positioned directly above the jar?

column4: >This keeps the exterior packaging clean, gives the QR generous real estate (the full flap width), and creates a natural discovery moment at first open. The consumer lifts the flap, sees strain name + QR, scans immediately before they even touch the jar. Key question: does DCR consider an inner flap sticker ""accessible to the consumer"" for the purpose of the product information QR requirement, and does it count as primary or secondary packaging surface?

column5: >Can we print the strain name in a branded typeface directly beneath the QR code on any packaging surface — lid label, testing label, or box sticker?

column4: >DCR restricts logos and imagery near QR codes but text labelling the product may be permitted. Clarify whether the strain name constitutes required labelling (always allowed) or discretionary branding (may require review). If permitted, this significantly improves scannability — consumers know what they're scanning before they point the camera.

column5: >What are the minimum and maximum allowable QR code dimensions under DCR, and does the regulation specify any quiet zone (white border) requirements?

column4: >A QR printed too small won't scan reliably, especially on curved surfaces like jar lids. General best practice is 2cm × 2cm minimum for reliable phone scanning at arm's length. Larger QRs on flat box surfaces can go up to the full available label area. Confirm whether state rules impose a cap, and whether colour inversion (light QR on dark background) is permitted.

column7: >Q4 — Inner flap sticker workflow for jar concentrates

column5: >For Head Change jar concentrates, the proposed placement is a sticker on the inside of the box top flap — printed with the batch QR code and strain name, applied before the jar goes in the box. What does this add to the packaging workflow and per-unit cost?

column4: >The sticker is applied once per box before sealing — likely the same station where the testing label goes on. Since the QR is batch-generated anyway, the sticker sheet is printed per batch run. Key questions: what is the flap interior dimensions for the current Head Change jar box, can the sticker printer handle the batch QR generation inline, and does the current line have a clean station for inner-flap application before the jar is placed?

column7: >Q5 — Is the printed QR on the testing label large enough to scan?

column5: >The printed QR on the testing label is the confirmed baseline for all products. The practical question is whether it is printed at a size and in a position that a consumer would actually notice and be able to scan reliably.

column4: >Testing labels are dense with required compliance fields. The QR may be small and hard to find. If the existing QR is already 2×2cm or larger and in a visible position, Tier 1 is ready to go with no changes. If it is smaller or buried in compliance text, the label template may need to be updated to make the QR larger and more prominent — or an additional consumer-facing placement (like an inner flap sticker) needs to be considered alongside it.

column5: >Action: get a testing label sample from packaging

column4: >Measure the current QR print size and test scannability at arm's length on each product format. This determines whether Tier 1 is already consumer-ready or needs a placement upgrade.

column1: The print strategy is confirmed: <strong style=""color:var(--white)
column2: >printed QR on the testing label, all products, all brands. The QR must route to a page that can authenticate the purchase — which means a pre-printed box QR is not viable. A box QR encodes a static URL that cannot be tied to a specific unit or batch at the point of printing, and cannot authenticate the individual purchase. The testing label already carries batch-specific data, making it the only packaging surface where a meaningful QR can be printed. Two remaining open actions: (1) confirm the printed QR is large enough to scan reliably, and (2) assess whether variable-data printing is feasible for the per-unit upgrade.

Every product ships with a testing label QR that routes to a batch-level verification page — confirming the product is from a legitimate, licensed Curador batch. This is Tier 1 and applies to all products with no exceptions. Tier 2 — a per-unit product ID sticker with a unique code per individual unit — enables deeper verification and unit-level loyalty tracking if state approval and logistics allow it.

column5: >Each testing label carries a QR encoded with that batch's unique code — e.g. <code style=""font-family:'DM Mono',monospace
column11: >headchange.com/verify/HC-2025-B047. No two batches share a code.

column4: >The URL is batch-specific, not product-category specific. Scanning a counterfeit or expired batch routes to an invalid page.

column5: >Landing page confirms: batch code is valid, batch is licensed and active, COA data for that specific run, strain/flavor details, and test date.

column4: >This is the DCR-required product info page. It satisfies the ""learn more about the product"" requirement for that exact unit — not a generic strain page.

column6: >Tier 1 batch code: Valid for the life of the batch run. All units from that run share the same QR destination. Multiple scans are expected and fine — a consumer can scan every time they open the jar.

column5: >Tier 2 unit code (if approved): Unique per individual unit. Enables true one-unit-one-code tracking. Scan volume monitoring becomes meaningful at the unit level — an unusually high scan count on a single product ID is a counterfeit signal worth flagging.
>Once the product is verified, the page transitions into the full strain/flavor card — COA, terpene profile, photos, brand story — and surfaces the loyalty join CTA.

column4: >Verification and loyalty capture happen in a single seamless flow. The consumer never sees two separate pages.

column1:
House of Brands · QR Journey Concept
Head Change · Safe Bet · Bubbles
Draft v1.2 · Revised for Batch Authentication
From Two QRs to One — Revised

The original Option D proposed two separate QRs: a compliance QR on the testing label and a marketing QR on the box. The current strategy supersedes this with a cleaner two-tier model. Tier 1 is the testing label QR — already on every product, batch-level, does compliance and loyalty in one scan. Tier 2 is a per-unit product ID sticker — a conditional upgrade if state permits and logistics allow, linking to an exact unit rather than a batch.

The key insight: the testing label QR is already unique per batch and already routes to the brand site. There is no need for a second generic marketing QR. The remaining decisions are (1) whether the testing label QR is large and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan, and (2) whether a Tier 2 per-unit product ID sticker is achievable — which would enable unit-level tracking and authentication beyond what a batch code can provide. Tier 1 ships. Tier 2 is pursued in parallel pending state and logistics confirmation.

column1: <title>Curador — House of Brands · QR Journey Concept</title>
One scan. One batch. Three brands. One ecosystem.

Every Curador product carries a single batch-specific QR code. It does three jobs in sequence: authenticates the product, surfaces COA data, and opens a loyalty capture moment — all in one scan, on a page unique to that batch.

01
Printed QR on Testing Label
Every product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. This is the confirmed touchpoint across all brands and all SKUs — no exceptions, no additional workflow. Batch-level code, routes to brand site, opens loyalty flow.

02
DCR Compliance
The testing label QR satisfies DCR's ""learn more about the product"" requirement. It routes to the brand's strain/flavor card with COA, potency, and terpene data for that batch.

03
First-Party Data
After landing on the strain card, a loyalty Join CTA and newsletter pop-up capture consumer data. Cookie-on members auto-log. No-cookie members see a sign-in prompt. One scan, one flow.

04
Unique Per-Unit QR — Upgrade Path
If in-house printing can support variable-data QR generation per unit, each product gets a unique code tied to that specific SKU. Enables unit-level authentication and richer loyalty tracking. Confirmed feasibility needed first.

column2: >Confirmed — Printed QR on testing label (all products): Every Curador product ships with a printed QR on the testing label. It routes to the brand site, surfaces COA data, and opens the loyalty flow. Batch-level code — the same QR for all units in a production run.

>Tier 2 — Per-product ID sticker (conditional upgrade): A separate sticker printed with a unique product-level QR code — one per individual unit, not per batch — enables unit-level authentication and tracking. This requires state approval and a logistics solution for per-unit sticker generation and application. If achievable, it is the superior experience. If not, Tier 1 already does the job.

column1: Before finalising the QR print strategy, two teams need answers. These questions are grouped by who owns them. The goal: find the path that maximises consumer journey without straining the packaging department.

column5: >Can we apply a sticker printed with the batch QR and strain name to the inside of the box top flap — visible the moment the consumer opens the box, positioned directly above the jar?

column4: >This keeps the exterior packaging clean, gives the QR generous real estate (the full flap width), and creates a natural discovery moment at first open. The consumer lifts the flap, sees strain name + QR, scans immediately before they even touch the jar. Key question: does DCR consider an inner flap sticker ""accessible to the consumer"" for the purpose of the product information QR requirement, and does it count as primary or secondary packaging surface?

column5: >Can we print the strain name in a branded typeface directly beneath the QR code on any packaging surface — lid label, testing label, or box sticker?

column4: >DCR restricts logos and imagery near QR codes but text labelling the product may be permitted. Clarify whether the strain name constitutes required labelling (always allowed) or discretionary branding (may require review). If permitted, this significantly improves scannability — consumers know what they're scanning before they point the camera.

column5: >What are the minimum and maximum allowable QR code dimensions under DCR, and does the regulation specify any quiet zone (white border) requirements?

column4: >A QR printed too small won't scan reliably, especially on curved surfaces like jar lids. General best practice is 2cm × 2cm minimum for reliable phone scanning at arm's length. Larger QRs on flat box surfaces can go up to the full available label area. Confirm whether state rules impose a cap, and whether colour inversion (light QR on dark background) is permitted.

column7: >Q4 — Inner flap sticker workflow for jar concentrates

column5: >For Head Change jar concentrates, the proposed placement is a sticker on the inside of the box top flap — printed with the batch QR code and strain name, applied before the jar goes in the box. What does this add to the packaging workflow and per-unit cost?

column4: >The sticker is applied once per box before sealing — likely the same station where the testing label goes on. Since the QR is batch-generated anyway, the sticker sheet is printed per batch run. Key questions: what is the flap interior dimensions for the current Head Change jar box, can the sticker printer handle the batch QR generation inline, and does the current line have a clean station for inner-flap application before the jar is placed?

column7: >Q5 — Is the printed QR on the testing label large enough to scan?

column5: >The printed QR on the testing label is the confirmed baseline for all products. The practical question is whether it is printed at a size and in a position that a consumer would actually notice and be able to scan reliably.

column4: >Testing labels are dense with required compliance fields. The QR may be small and hard to find. If the existing QR is already 2×2cm or larger and in a visible position, Tier 1 is ready to go with no changes. If it is smaller or buried in compliance text, the label template may need to be updated to make the QR larger and more prominent — or an additional consumer-facing placement (like an inner flap sticker) needs to be considered alongside it.

column5: >Action: get a testing label sample from packaging

column4: >Measure the current QR print size and test scannability at arm's length on each product format. This determines whether Tier 1 is already consumer-ready or needs a placement upgrade.

column1: The print strategy is confirmed: <strong style=""color:var(--white)
column2: >printed QR on the testing label, all products, all brands. The QR must route to a page that can authenticate the purchase — which means a pre-printed box QR is not viable. A box QR encodes a static URL that cannot be tied to a specific unit or batch at the point of printing, and cannot authenticate the individual purchase. The testing label already carries batch-specific data, making it the only packaging surface where a meaningful QR can be printed. Two remaining open actions: (1) confirm the printed QR is large enough to scan reliably, and (2) assess whether variable-data printing is feasible for the per-unit upgrade.

Every product ships with a testing label QR that routes to a batch-level verification page — confirming the product is from a legitimate, licensed Curador batch. This is Tier 1 and applies to all products with no exceptions. Tier 2 — a per-unit product ID sticker with a unique code per individual unit — enables deeper verification and unit-level loyalty tracking if state approval and logistics allow it.

column1: Two Ways to Verify. One Reward.

Consumers can verify their Curador purchase and earn loyalty points through two paths. Path A — scanning the testing label QR — is confirmed and operational. Path B — uploading a dispensary receipt — is a proposed upgrade path, pending confirmation that Missouri dispensary receipts include a product ID number that can be matched against Curador's product registry, and that OCR parsing of those receipts is reliable enough to automate verification.

column2: >Must confirm before receipt upload can proceed:

column3: >Do Missouri dispensary POS systems print a product ID number on receipts? This is the single most critical dependency. Without a product ID on the receipt, there is no reliable way to authenticate the specific product purchased — product name alone is too ambiguous and too easy to spoof. Talk to dispensary partners and pull sample receipts from multiple MO retailers to confirm.

>Can OCR reliably read that product ID from a phone photo of a receipt? Cannabis receipts vary significantly by POS system (Dutchie, Treez, Flowhub, BioTrack). Some print cleanly, some are thermal and fade. Test OCR accuracy across real receipt samples before committing to automated processing.

From Two QRs to One — Revised

The original Option D proposed two separate QRs: a compliance QR on the testing label and a marketing QR on the box. The current strategy supersedes this with a cleaner two-tier model. Tier 1 is the testing label QR — already on every product, batch-level, does compliance and loyalty in one scan. Tier 2 is a per-unit product ID sticker — a conditional upgrade if state permits and logistics allow, linking to an exact unit rather than a batch.

The key insight: the testing label QR is already unique per batch and already routes to the brand site. There is no need for a second generic marketing QR. The remaining decisions are (1) whether the testing label QR is large and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan, and (2) whether a Tier 2 per-unit product ID sticker is achievable — which would enable unit-level tracking and authentication beyond what a batch code can provide. Tier 1 ships. Tier 2 is pursued in parallel pending state and logistics confirmation.

column5: >The testing label already carries a QR for DCR compliance — but it's small, hard to scan, and not on retail packaging.
>A strain-specific sticker QR solves visibility but adds a separate print run and another touchpoint in the packaging workflow for every small batch.

column4: >A box-printed QR is clean and bulk-ordered but can't point to a specific strain — only a broader SKU page.

column5: >Let the two QRs do different jobs. The testing label QR handles compliance — strain-specific, required anyway, done. The box-printed QR handles marketing — visible, bulk-friendly, always lands on the brand site.

>The box QR routes to a brand SKU overview page where the consumer selects their strain or flavor. Since they just bought the product, they know what they have — one tap to confirm is engagement, not friction.

column4: >Either way, the consumer lands on the brand site. Data capture opportunity exists on both paths.

column5: >The SKU overview page becomes a live surface — showing current available strains, seasonal drops, what's in stock right now. More useful than a static strain card for repeat buyers.

column4: >Newsletter pop-up fires on the overview page before they navigate to their strain — another clean capture moment that doesn't require a full loyalty commitment.
>Exact strain/flavor card on brand site — COA, potency, terpenes, strain story.

column1: Both QRs always route to the brand site — consumer lands on Curador-owned digital real estate on every scan regardless of which code they use. The testing label QR satisfies the regulator. The box QR builds the business.

Every product ships with a testing label QR that routes to a batch-level verification page — confirming the product is from a legitimate, licensed Curador batch. This is Tier 1 and applies to all products with no exceptions. Tier 2 — a per-unit product ID sticker with a unique code per individual unit — enables deeper verification and unit-level loyalty tracking if state approval and logistics allow it.

column5: >Each testing label carries a QR encoded with that batch's unique code — e.g. <code style=""font-family:'DM Mono',monospace
column11: >headchange.com/verify/HC-2025-B047. No two batches share a code.

column4: >The URL is batch-specific, not product-category specific. Scanning a counterfeit or expired batch routes to an invalid page.

column5: >Landing page confirms: batch code is valid, batch is licensed and active, COA data for that specific run, strain/flavor details, and test date.

column4: >This is the DCR-required product info page. It satisfies the ""learn more about the product"" requirement for that exact unit — not a generic strain page.

column6: >Tier 1 batch code: Valid for the life of the batch run. All units from that run share the same QR destination. Multiple scans are expected and fine — a consumer can scan every time they open the jar.

column5: >Tier 2 unit code (if approved): Unique per individual unit. Enables true one-unit-one-code tracking. Scan volume monitoring becomes meaningful at the unit level — an unusually high scan count on a single product ID is a counterfeit signal worth flagging.
>Once the product is verified, the page transitions into the full strain/flavor card — COA, terpene profile, photos, brand story — and surfaces the loyalty join CTA.

column4: >Verification and loyalty capture happen in a single seamless flow. The consumer never sees two separate pages.

column2: >Batch code generated at production · Encoded into QR at print · Registered in backend before product ships
Is the current testing label QR large enough and visible enough for consumers to discover and scan?
Does the existing QR already route to the brand site, or does that URL need to be configured?
Where is batch COA data registered — internal CMS or testing lab system — and can it be pulled by batch code?

Does state allow an additional consumer-facing QR sticker on packaging beyond the required testing label? Confirm with <a href=""/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection"" class=""__cf_email__"" data-cfemail=""5d1e3c33333c3f342e0d2f3239283e291e32302d31343c333e381d35383c312935733032733a322b"">[email&#160
Can our label printer generate unique variable-data QR codes per unit at production speed?
What serialisation system registers each product ID before it ships — internal CMS, Alpine IQ, or dedicated backend?
What is the per-unit cost difference between batch stickers and per-unit variable-data stickers?
Scan volume monitoring for counterfeit detection — meaningful only at Tier 2 unit level. Who gets alerted and at what threshold?

column1: Two Ways to Verify. One Reward.

Consumers can verify their Curador purchase and earn loyalty points through two paths. Path A — scanning the testing label QR — is confirmed and operational. Path B — uploading a dispensary receipt — is a proposed upgrade path, pending confirmation that Missouri dispensary receipts include a product ID number that can be matched against Curador's product registry, and that OCR parsing of those receipts is reliable enough to automate verification.

>Routes to brand verification page — batch or unit code confirmed as legitimate
>Strain card loads — COA, potency, terpenes, brand story
>Loyalty CTA appears — join or log product to earn points. Cookie-on members auto-log instantly.
>At point of purchase · First open · Members auto-logging
>Consumer lands on any brand page — via QR, direct visit, or link — and sees the receipt upload CTA
>They photograph or upload their dispensary receipt — standard paper or digital receipt both accepted

column5: >product ID number, and purchase date — the product ID on the receipt must match a registered Curador product ID in the backend.
column6: >⚐ Requires confirmation that MO dispensary receipts include a scannable product ID.

column4: >Purchase verified — points awarded to loyalty account. Same earn as QR path. Receipt logged to prevent double-claiming.
>Post-purchase · When QR not scanned at point of sale · Existing customers discovering loyalty late

column7: >Why receipt upload would matter — if confirmed

column4: >The testing label QR may be small or not obviously consumer-facing. Many buyers won't see it until they're already home. Receipt upload captures this group.
>Every legal cannabis sale generates a receipt. This is a universal proof-of-purchase touchpoint that exists regardless of QR placement, label size, or packaging format.
>A customer who bought two weeks ago and just found the loyalty program can upload their receipt and still earn. QR scanning can't do this — the receipt can.
>Receipt uploads tell us which dispensaries are moving which products. This is first-party retail intelligence that QR scans can't provide — receipts show where the purchase happened.

column7: >Verification logic — how each path confirms a real purchase

column1: — Batch code in QR URL matches a registered Curador batch in backend
— Batch is marked as active (not recalled, not expired)
— Brand and product type confirmed from batch record

column2: >Tier 2: Unit code matched to a specific serialised product ID

column1: — Points awarded immediately on confirmed match

column2: >Product ID matched against Curador product registry — this is the authentication step. Product name alone is not sufficient.

column1: — Purchase date within valid claim window
— Receipt hash stored to prevent duplicate claims
— Points awarded on confirmed product ID match

column2: >⚐ Entire path depends on: (1) MO dispensary receipts including a product ID, and (2) OCR reliably reading that ID from a photo

column7: >Where the receipt upload CTA would appear — pending confirmation

column4: >Below the loyalty Join CTA — secondary option for anyone who has the product but didn't scan the QR at the right moment.
>Logged-in members can upload receipts for any past purchase within the claim window — retroactive earning for missed scans.
>Post sign-up comms include a ""missed a scan? upload your receipt"" CTA — captures purchases made before the consumer enrolled.
>Points redemption hub also surfaces receipt upload — consumer arrives to redeem and can top up their balance before checking out.

column2: >Must confirm before receipt upload can proceed:

column3: >Do Missouri dispensary POS systems print a product ID number on receipts? This is the single most critical dependency. Without a product ID on the receipt, there is no reliable way to authenticate the specific product purchased — product name alone is too ambiguous and too easy to spoof. Talk to dispensary partners and pull sample receipts from multiple MO retailers to confirm.

>Can OCR reliably read that product ID from a phone photo of a receipt? Cannabis receipts vary significantly by POS system (Dutchie, Treez, Flowhub, BioTrack). Some print cleanly, some are thermal and fade. Test OCR accuracy across real receipt samples before committing to automated processing.

Head Change Pilot — Scalable Across Portfolio
A controlled, compliance-safe communication engine for CURADOR brands
Own the audience → communicate with restraint → drive behavior through culture, not pressure
Safe, scalable, brand-building. Weekly or biweekly cadence. This is where the relationship lives.
Used sparingly. High intent only — drops, availability, VIP access. Never noise.
This week's release — framed as discovery, not hard sell
Music, art, influence — aligned with brand DNA
""From the counter"" — builds trust and compliance safety
""New Head Change release just landed near you""
""You're on the early list — tap to see what just dropped""
User clicks → landing page → email/SMS opt-in
Weekly email content builds the relationship
✓ Clear opt-in for SMS
We don't start by talking to consumers directly. We start by leveraging the relationships dispensaries already have — then gradually earn the right to own the conversation ourselves.
Partner with dispensaries through Alpine IQ to reach their existing customer base. These are warm, high-intent consumers already in the store ecosystem.
Campaign traffic lands on a CURADOR-branded page — not the dispensary's. We own the destination from day one.
Light opt-in offer. Collect email as the primary asset. Begin sending brand awareness content — not product pushes. The goal is list growth and trust.
As the list grows, we reduce reliance on Alpine IQ campaigns and begin communicating directly with our own audience. The brand voice takes over.
The team activates its own acquisition channels — in-house rewards programs and on-site email capture to grow the list independently of dispensary partnerships.
CURADOR Signal launches. Drop alerts begin via SMS. Audience segmentation kicks in — consumers, budtenders, and early VIPs start receiving tailored content.
Behavior-based automation kicks in. Welcome flows, re-engagement sequences, and VIP early access become self-running systems.
Location-aware SMS and email campaigns tie availability directly to nearby dispensaries. The right message reaches the right person in the right market.
The system built on Head Change becomes the template. Every brand in the CURADOR portfolio inherits the infrastructure, the playbook, and the audience.
This is not a cannabis promo system
A controlled, culturally-driven communication channel that converts attention into loyalty
